perturbation.original_text: a) that it shall treat the Confidential Information directly or indirectly disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party as strictly confidential and will not without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party disclose any part of the Confidential Information to any third party, except where compelled by law or regulation;
perturbation.changed_text: a) that it shall treat the Confidential Information directly or indirectly disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party as strictly confidential and will not without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party disclose any part of the Confidential Information to any third party;
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exception for disclosures compelled by law or regulation could force a Receiving Party to violate mandatory legal reporting requirements, such as those related to anti-corruption or financial disclosures in Norway.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Act of 19 June 1964 No. 14 relating to the Public Administration (Forvaltningsloven) § 13d
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13d mandates the disclosure of information when required by law, regulations, or court decisions. Removing the 'except where compelled by law or regulation' clause forces the receiving party to potentially withhold legally required disclosures, contradicting Norwegian law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Simmons & Simmons We're sorry but website doesn't work properly without JavaScript enabled. Please enable it to continue.
perturbation.justification: The original text includes an exception for disclosing confidential information when compelled by law or regulation. The changed text removes this exception. The Law Explanation states this removal forces the receiving party to potentially withhold legally required disclosures, contradicting Norwegian law. The Perturbation Explanation specifies that this could force a Receiving Party to violate mandatory legal reporting requirements. This creates a legal contradiction because the modified contract now requires the receiving party to potentially violate legally mandated reporting, as suggested by the Perturbation and Law Explanations.

perturbation.original_text: Clause 13 The NDA shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws and regulations of Norway.
perturbation.changed_text: Clause 13 The NDA shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the principles of international commerce.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Norway to 'principles of international commerce' removes mandatory protections afforded under Norwegian law, potentially impacting consumer rights or data protection requirements for Norwegian entities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Act of 15 June 2018 No. 40 relating to the processing of personal data (Personal Data Act) (Personopplysningsloven)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Personopplysningsloven implements GDPR in Norway, providing data protection rights to individuals. By switching the governing law, the contract attempts to circumvent these mandatory data protection regulations, potentially leading to non-compliance and legal penalties.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specified Norwegian law as the governing law. The changed text replaces this with 'principles of international commerce.' According to the Law Explanation, Norwegian law implements GDPR, providing data protection rights. The perturbation removes mandatory protections afforded under Norwegian law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows the Personopplysningsloven, which implements GDPR in Norway. Therefore, changing the governing law attempts to circumvent mandatory data protection regulations, leading to non-compliance. This is a direct contradiction, making the altered clause likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Clause 14 Any disputes between the Parties arising out of or in connection with the NDA and which cannot be settled amicably shall be settled in the common court of law. The legal venue is Oslo tingrett, Norway.
perturbation.changed_text: Clause 14 Any disputes between the Parties arising out of or in connection with the NDA and which cannot be settled amicably shall be settled by binding arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the dispute resolution from Norwegian courts to binding arbitration may limit access to statutory remedies and judicial review available under Norwegian law, potentially harming one party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Act relating to Arbitration (Voldgiftsloven) of 14 May 2004 No. 25 § 37
perturbation.law_explanation: While arbitration is permitted in Norway, the Voldgiftsloven provides a framework for judicial review in specific instances (e.g., procedural errors). Replacing court resolution with *binding* arbitration may improperly prevent a party from appealing procedural errors and seeking remedies available under Norwegian law and its judicial system.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Retsinformation You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies dispute resolution in a common court of law, explicitly naming Oslo tingrett, Norway. The changed text mandates binding arbitration, omitting the court venue and potentially limiting judicial review. The Law Explanation points out that while arbitration is permitted, the change to *binding* arbitration might prevent appealing procedural errors. The "Omission Legal Contradiction" definition states that a legal contradiction arises when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not helpful and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, the Perturbation Explanation states that changing the dispute resolution from Norwegian courts to binding arbitration may limit access to statutory remedies and judicial review available under Norwegian law, potentially harming one party. This constitutes an omission of the protections afforded by the Norwegian legal system, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Clause 5 Either Party shall, upon 30 days written request by the other Party, return to the other party all Confidential Information which the latter has disclosed to the former, together with all copies or reproductions thereof together with a confirmation in writing that the Confidential Information in computerized form has been destroyed. Furthermore, at the request of the Disclosing Party the Receiving Party shall immediately cease to use the Confidential Information for any purposes whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: Clause 5 Either Party shall, upon request by the other Party, return to the other party all Confidential Information which the latter has disclosed to the former, together with all copies or reproductions thereof. Furthermore, at the request of the Disclosing Party the Receiving Party shall cease to use the Confidential Information for any purposes whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 30-day notice period and the requirement for written confirmation of data destruction could be seen as a violation of data protection laws in Norway, especially concerning the 'right to be forgotten' principles under GDPR.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Act of 15 June 2018 No. 40 relating to the processing of personal data (Personal Data Act) (Personopplysningsloven) § 17
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17 of the Personopplysningsloven, which reflects Article 17 of the GDPR (Right to Erasure), requires prompt action to delete personal data. Omitting the 30 days' written notice could unduly delay compliance with erasure requests.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Personvernombud | Datatilsynet Administrer cookies Vi bruker informasjonskapsler (cookies) Våre nettsider benytter noen få informasjonskapsler (cookies) til sikkerhet. I tillegg bruker vi cookies til en valgfri skjemafunksjon der vi ber om samtykke først. Les om hvilke vi bruker og hvordan vi administrerer dem i cookie-erklæringen vår . Nødvendige cookies Disse støtter opp under kjernefunksjonalitet knyttet til sikkerhet. Vi har vurdert disse som nødvendige, og de lagres derfor uten samtykke. Skjemafunksjoner Dette er nødvendig å samtykke til dersom du ønsker å ta i bruk skjema på nettsidene. Den øvrige funksjonaliteten på nettsidene påvirkes ikke dersom du lar være å samtykke. Valget du tar her gjelder i inntil 90 dager. Skjemafunksjoner av/på Du kan trekke tilbake samtykket når som helst ved å velge «administrer cookies» nederst på våre sider. Lagre mitt valg Lukk Personvernombud Hvem må ha personvernombud? Personvernombudets oppgaver Hvilke kvalifikasjoner trenger et personvernombud? Registrering av personvernombud Hvordan tilrettelegge for personvernombudets arbeid? Hvordan sikre ombudets uavhengighet? Oversikt over registrerte personvernombud Nyttige nettverk og studietilbud Datatilsynets kontaktperson for personvernombud Personvernrådgiver i politiet og påtalemyndigheten Personvernombud Personvernombudene er sentrale i virksomhetenes etterlevelse av personvernlovgivningen, og skal ha en uavhengig rolle. Ombudets rolle er å gi råd til ledelsen og andre i virksomheten, men også å kontrollere etterlevelsen av regelverket. I tillegg skal de være kontaktpunkt for de registrerte og Datatilsynet. På disse sidene har vi samlet all relevant informasjon om denne ombudsrollen. Personvernombud eller ikke? Er du usikker på om virksomheten din har plikt til å ha personvernombud etter personvernregelverket? Vi har laget en forenklet trinn-for-trinn-veiledning for å prøve å hjelpe deg. Finn ut om du må ha personvernombud her Tips til nye ombud Vi har samlet noen tips og råd til hvordan personvernombudet skal gå frem for å skaffe seg oversikten de trenger, holde seg oppdaterte og ikke minst gjøre seg synlige og tilgjengelige i virksomheten - både for de ansvarlige, de ansatte og de registrerte. Tips til personvernombud Forskning og personvernombud I noen sammenhenger kan en virksomhet behandle særlige kategorier av personopplysninger (også kalt sensitive personopplysninger) uten samtykke . I disse tilfellene har den behandlingsansvarlige plikt til å rådføre seg med personvernombudet. Les mer om forskning og personvernombud Relevante bestemmelser og kilder Personvernforordningen artikkel 37, 38 og 39 (lovdata.no) Personopplysningsloven § 9, 10 og 11 (lovdata.no) Artikkel 29-gruppen sin veiledning om personvernombud (EU-kommisjonens nettside, engelsk) Relevant innhold Last ned personvernombudets logo Personvernombud - undersøkelse 2025
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides a 30-day written notice period for returning confidential information and requires written confirmation of data destruction. The changed text removes both of these requirements. The Law Explanation states that Section 17 of the Personopplysningsloven (reflecting GDPR Article 17) necessitates prompt action for deleting personal data, implying the removed 30-day notice could cause undue delays. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Personopplysningsloven § 17 regarding exceptions to data subject rights, indirectly highlighting the importance of these rights, which are undermined by the omission. The omission of the 30 day notice and written confirmation of destruction creates a contradiction because it removes consumer protections relating to the 'right to be forgotten' under GDPR and Personopplysningsloven, and introduces a delay in compliance with data erasure requests.
